theorem_title: Lean Eval: Rouche theorem via log-counting zeros
theorem_statement: Let f and g be complex functions with f meromorphic and g analytic on the whole complex plane. If on the circle of radius R the norm of g is strictly less than the norm of f, then the log-counting function of f + g at radius R equals that of f.
raw_proof_text:
For each t in [0,1], define h_t(z) = f(z) + t g(z). First observe that h_t has no zeros on the boundary circle |z| = R. Indeed, if |z| = R, then |t g(z)| <= |g(z)| < |f(z)|. If f(z) + t g(z) = 0, then |f(z)| = |t g(z)| <= |g(z)|, contradicting the strict boundary inequality.

Define N(t) = (1 / (2 pi i)) times the contour integral over the boundary circle of h_t'(z) / h_t(z) dz. Since h_t has no boundary zeros, the integrand is continuous on the boundary and depends continuously on t. Hence N(t) is continuous in t.

By the argument principle, N(t) is exactly the number of zeros of h_t inside the disk, counted with multiplicity. In the Lean Eval statement this zero count is represented by the value-distribution/log-counting formalism. In particular, N(t) is integer-valued for each t. A continuous map from the connected interval [0,1] into the discrete integers must be constant. Therefore N(0) = N(1). Since h_0 = f and h_1 = f + g, f and f + g have the same zero count inside the disk.

For the argument-principle justification, if h_t is holomorphic near the closed disk and has no zeros on the boundary, then its zeros in the disk are finite. Factor h_t as product_j (z - alpha_j)^{r_j} times a nonvanishing holomorphic factor u. Taking logarithmic derivatives gives h_t'/h_t = sum_j r_j/(z - alpha_j) + u'/u. Integrating around the boundary, each simple pole term contributes 2 pi i times its multiplicity, while u'/u contributes zero. Thus the integral counts zeros with multiplicity.

Likely Formal Islands graph: define the homotopy h_t; prove boundary nonvanishing from the strict inequality; define the logarithmic zero count; prove it is continuous in t; use integer-valued continuity to show it is constant; identify endpoints t = 0 and t = 1. The best islands are the boundary nonvanishing inequality and the integer-valued continuous function is constant step. The argument-principle/log-counting infrastructure is likely the remaining burden.
